---
title: "Escaping Inequality in India: The Role of English-Medium Instruction in Local Development"
bibliography: ../../paper/references.bib
citeproc: true
suppress-bibliography: true
format:
  poster-typst:
    size: "36x24"
    poster-authors: "Rishav Roy"
    departments: "Department of Economics, University of Wisconsin-Madison"
    institution-logo: "../../assets/uw-logo-horizontal-full-color-print.pdf"
    footer-text: "PREDOC.org Research Conference | Chicago Booth | July 30-31, 2026"
    footer-url: "rishavbroy.github.io"
    footer-emails: "roybrishav@gmail.com"
    footer-color: "7a0019"
    univ-logo-column-size: 8
    title-column-size: 22
    title-font-size: 42
    authors-font-size: 28
    footer-url-font-size: 20
    footer-text-font-size: 24
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Why this matters

1. *Jobless growth.* Rapid GDP growth has coincided with weak wage growth and poor employment prospects for educated young people [@organization2024a].
2. *English as human capital.* English fluency is associated with wage premia, higher education, and service-sector employment [@azam2013a; @chakraborty2016a].
3. *EMI joins schooling and English.* English-medium instruction (EMI) may expand opportunity, but fees, school quality, and unequal access may limit its benefits [@lahoti2019a; @bhattacharyaMediatingInequalitiesExploring2013].
4. *Question.* Does greater district EMI enrollment predict faster household-consumption growth over the next decade?

# Research design

```{=typst}
#block(fill: rgb("f3f0ea"), inset: 10pt, radius: 5pt, width: 100%)[
  #grid(columns: (1fr, auto, 1fr, auto, 1fr), gutter: 7pt,
    align(center)[*Census 2001*\ Mother tongues\ + district identities],
    align(center)[#text(size: 24pt, fill: rgb("7a0019"))[→]],
    align(center)[*NSS 2007-08*\ EMI exposure\ + baseline controls],
    align(center)[#text(size: 24pt, fill: rgb("7a0019"))[→]],
    align(center)[*NSS 2017-18*\ Consumption\ + inequality outcomes]
  )
  #v(7pt)
  #align(center)[*Common unit:* Census 2001 districts]
]
```

**Treatment:** district EMI enrollment among school-going children in 2007-08. **Outcome:** growth in mean household consumption through 2017-18. **Instrument:** linguistic distance from Hindi, based on each district's three leading mother tongues. **Controls:** baseline living standards, demography, social composition, landholding, head education, and region fixed effects.

![District EMI exposure in 2007-08.](../../outputs/figures/main/map_emi_exposure.pdf){width=76%}


# Main result

![Adjusted consumption growth across observed district EMI exposure. Points mark exposure percentiles; the ribbon shows 95% confidence intervals.](../../outputs/figures/main/poster_emie_expected_values.pdf){width=92%}

The estimate is small and imprecise: higher EMI exposure does not clearly predict faster local consumption growth after baseline adjustment. Individual gains may still matter without raising average living standards in students' home districts.

# Interpretation

- **Selection:** EMI enrollment reflects family resources, access, and school supply.
- **Migration:** gains may follow students who leave their childhood districts.
- **School quality:** an English label need not imply English instruction [@lahoti2019a].
- **Labor demand:** returns depend on local firms and occupations.

# Instrumental variation

![Average linguistic distance from Hindi, constructed from Census 2001 mother-tongue composition.](../../outputs/figures/main/map_linguistic_distance.pdf){width=72%}

Conditional on baseline characteristics and region, the design assumes linguistic distance affects later consumption only through EMI exposure.


# Why district histories matter

## Name resolution

Resolve typographical errors, punctuation, transliteration, shortening, and official renaming for both district and state names [@indiastatestories1HowDistricts2025].

## Geographical change

- **Partition:** one district becomes two or more.
- **Merger:** two or more districts become one.
- **Carve-out or boundary shift:** territory is reassigned across districts.

Equal names across years do not prove equal territory.

## Variable assignment

- *Counts:* sum additive components.
- *Means and shares, including EMI:* pool records or numerators and denominators, then recompute.
- *Gini coefficients:* pool household records and recompute; never average district Ginis.

Where exact assignment is impossible, retain deterministic histories and report alternative assumptions.

# Contribution

The project integrates household surveys, Census language data, administrative histories, and spatial data in a reproducible district panel. Estimates remain provisional while district histories are strengthened.

```{=typst}
#block(fill: rgb("f3f0ea"), inset: 6pt, radius: 5pt, width: 100%)[
  #grid(columns: (1fr, auto), gutter: 14pt,
    [*Code, paper, and updates*\ #link("https://git.new/FwmVaXq")[git.new/FwmVaXq]\ #link("https://rishavbroy.github.io")[rishavbroy.github.io]],
    image("../../assets/repo-qr.svg", width: 0.85in)
  )
]
```
